planting.note: Sow 4–8 weeks before first fall frost, or in early spring; fixes nitrogen and blooms crimson.

directions: Broadcast seed onto a raked bed at about 1/4 in depth and keep moist until established. Let it grow through winter, then mow and turn it under a few weeks before planting to release its fixed nitrogen. Terminate at early bloom for the best nitrogen return.

planting.note: Sow from 6 weeks before to just after first frost; the most cold-hardy cover crop, germinating in near-freezing soil.

directions: Broadcast seed at about 1/2 in depth in fall; it germinates in cold soil when little else will. It scavenges leftover nitrogen and smothers weeds over winter, then puts on fast spring growth. Mow and turn under 2–3 weeks before planting, as its residue briefly suppresses small seeds.

planting.note: Sow 4–8 weeks before first frost, often mixed with winter rye which gives it something to climb.

directions: Sow seed about 1/2 in deep, ideally inoculated with the correct rhizobia and paired with a grass like rye for support. It overwinters and fixes a large amount of nitrogen by spring bloom. Terminate at early flowering for maximum nitrogen and easy breakdown.

planting.note: Sow anytime in warm frost-free weather; a fast summer smother crop that flowers in ~5 weeks.

directions: Broadcast seed about 1/2 in deep into warm soil; it sprouts within days and shades out weeds quickly. Cut or turn it under at first bloom, before it sets seed, or it will reseed. It also draws pollinators and helps make soil phosphorus available.

planting.note: Sow 4–8 weeks before frost in fall, or in earliest spring; nitrogen-fixing and edible tender shoots.

directions: Sow inoculated seed about 1 in deep, often mixed with oats or rye for support. The vines fix nitrogen and produce edible pea shoots you can harvest before turning the crop under. Terminate at flowering for the best nitrogen contribution.

planting.note: Sow 6–10 weeks before first frost; the deep taproot breaks up compacted soil and winter-kills in cold zones.

directions: Broadcast seed about 1/2 in deep in late summer; the thick taproot drills deep and shatters compaction. In cold-winter areas it dies and rots in place, leaving channels and released nutrients. Where it survives, mow before it flowers to prevent seeding.
